what_it_does: It is a living reorganisation blueprint and progress log for the counterpart/design repository, mapping the intended six-territory structure, the DNA-system principles, current proof-suite status, known drift, staged work plan, path rewrites, open decisions, and per-stage verification gates.
observations: The top of the file is a progress log that records completed stages with commit hashes and verification results.
It defines six first-class territories: registry, dna, surface, app, server, and pieces.
It lists a proof suite with per-proof status and specific failure details.
It contains a corrected drift list of 22 structural issues, each with evidence and blocking impact.
It adds sections on the graph instrument and the DNA↔composition merge seam.
It outlines five unification workstreams labeled A through E.
It provides a corrected staged build sequence from Stage 0 to Stage 9.
It includes a target directory tree and a detailed path-rewrite map for atomic commits.
It ends with an open-decisions list explicitly directed at Tim.
It references DNA-stream identifiers, board item IDs, and specific commit hashes throughout.
standouts: The file mixes future blueprint, live progress log, and hand-off record in one document.
It treats the repository as a Design DNA Pack governed by laws such as derive-never-place and registry-is-truth.
It locks a division of labor between a structural stream and a DNA stream.
It declares the reorg closed while still enumerating remaining DNA-stream work.
conventions: Uses markdown headers, tables, and code-formatted paths.
Labels stages and sub-slices with codes like E.1, E.2, E.3b.
Cites commit hashes and branch/stream identifiers as evidence.
Cites external board item IDs for cross-repo seams.
Combines narrative explanation with checklists, drift tables, and verification tables.
concerns: The progress log and remaining-work sections overlap, so the same item can appear both done and pending.
Some status snapshots are dated 2026-06-17 while later close-out claims may supersede them, risking internal inconsistency.
The open-decisions list is long and some items are described as decided elsewhere without being struck through.
The document is very long and densely cross-referenced, making it easy for a single edit to desynchronise sections.
notes: This is the repo's self-maintained reorganisation war-room document: it records what was moved, why, what still depends on the DNA stream, and the laws the structure is supposed to obey.
questions: Which items listed under REMAINING are actually still open versus already completed in later progress-log entries?
What is the exact current file tree after the close-out commits?
How do the DNA-stream identifiers map to actual branches or authors?
Now that both group and kind/demonstrates exist in the manifest, which field drives rail grouping?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to be a living map and blueprint; the file does exactly that, while also functioning as a commit-by-commit progress log and hand-off record.
apparent_themes: Separation of structural and content/DNA work streams.
intention_signals: It is trying to hold the whole reorganisation in one readable artifact so that every move can be traced to a principle, a stage, a verification gate, and a responsible stream.
novelty: It embeds a live progress log with commit hashes inside a design document and explicitly models cross-repo merge seams as first-class structural concerns.
contribution: It provides a single self-contained record of the reorganisation rationale, stage sequence, path mappings, verification criteria, and open decisions that a fused system would need to preserve for traceability.
summary_for_embedding: A living reorganisation blueprint and progress log for the counterpart/design repository, documenting the six-territory target structure, DNA-system principles, proof-suite status, staged migration plan, path rewrites, and open decisions.
intention_for_embedding: To coordinate a large repository reorganisation by mapping current state, target territories, workstreams, atomic moves, verification gates, and remaining decisions.
